ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Nasi Kunyit; 'rice dish' (FOODON_00002422) is used as the closest parent. A more specific term for glutinous rice dishes cooked with coconut milk and turmeric is not available in FoodOn as of 2026-04-17.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Nasi Kunyit; 'Rice dish' (A0012X) is used as the closest parent. A more specific code for glutinous rice dishes cooked with coconut milk and turmeric is not available in FoodEx2 as of 2026-04-17.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for Nasi Kunyit; 'Rice dish (food)' (286500006) is used as the closest parent. A more specific code for this traditional Malay preparation is not available in SNOMED CT as of 2026-04-17.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Nasi Kunyit is one plate, approximately 150 grams, often served with curries or rendang.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data directly from HPB Singapore for 'Nasi Kunyit'. All core macros and available micronutrients are sourced from this entry.

gi_source.title: Glutinous rice (sticky rice) — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI for Nasi Kunyit is estimated based on glutinous rice (GI ~98), its primary carbohydrate ingredient, sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. The addition of coconut milk and turmeric in typical preparation quantities is not expected to materially lower the GI of the glutinous rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (98 × 31.69 × 1.5) / 100 = 46.6g for a 150g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Borderline sodium content combined with very low potassium
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is borderline at 235mg/100g with very low potassium (~55mg/100g) providing minimal counter-balance; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, avoid high-sodium accompaniments, and pair with potassium-rich foods such as banana or spinach-based dishes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high glycemic index (~98) and high carbohydrate content with negligible dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Glutinous rice has an extremely high GI (~98) and provides 31.7g carbohydrate per 100g with only 0.14g dietary fibre, causing rapid and significant postprandial blood glucose spikes; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish or consume only a very small portion as an occasional treat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content with anti-inflammatory turmeric component
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat is low at approximately 1.1g/100g and trans fat is absent; turmeric (curcumin) provides anti-inflammatory benefit, but individuals with CVD should remain mindful of total saturated fat from accompanying dishes and request reduced coconut milk preparation where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric offset partially by pro-inflammatory potential of high glycemic index
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric (curcumin) used in material quantity provides evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit relevant to RA, but the very high GI of glutinous rice can promote systemic inflammation; consume in moderation and consider adding black pepper to enhance curcumin bioavailability.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Kunyit, also known as Malay turmeric rice or Pulut Kunyit, is a traditional Malay dish made from glutinous (sticky) rice cooked with coconut milk and fresh turmeric, which gives it a distinctive golden-yellow colour and a warm, earthy aroma. The rice is typically soaked overnight, then steamed or boiled with coconut milk (santan) and turmeric until soft, sticky, and fragrant. It has a rich, mildly sweet, and savoury taste with a tender, chewy texture characteristic of glutinous rice. Culturally, Nasi Kunyit is closely associated with Malay festive occasions such as weddings, engagements, and birth celebrations, and is commonly served alongside chicken rendang, beef curry, or sambal. Nutritionally, it provides 151 kcal per 100g, with 31.7g carbohydrate, 3.0g protein, and 1.32g fat. Its very high glycemic index (~98) makes it a significant concern for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium at 235mg/100g is borderline for hypertension. Turmeric contributes curcumin, which has documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Is Nasi Kunyit suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient data is sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for 'Nasi Kunyit', which is an approved primary regional source. GI is estimated from glutinous rice via the University of Sydney GI Database, an approved GI-specific source, with the analogous estimation basis clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for Nasi Kunyit are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as of 2026-04-17. Potassium value of 55mg/100g is an estimate based on analogous glutinous rice preparations from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, as HPB does not report potassium for this entry. Saturated fat of 1.1g/100g is estimated from the coconut milk component based on USDA FoodData Central data for coconut milk, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.
